commit aa8fa415c61507b8e7c4e7a368a371092f7ab661
Author: Muhammad Usama Anjum <usama.anjum@arm.com>

mm/page_alloc: Optimize __free_contig_frozen_range()

This commit optimizes the frozen page freeing path by replacing the
individual page freeing loop with a call to __free_contig_range_common().
This aims to improve performance by batching and coalescing prepared
pages into higher-order blocks.

> diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
> index 9ea2765f5aea..b969192589fb 100644
> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
> @@ -7076,8 +7076,7 @@ static int __alloc_contig_verify_gfp_mask(gfp_t gfp_mask, gfp_t *gfp_cc_mask)
>  
>  static void __free_contig_frozen_range(unsigned long pfn, unsigned long nr_pages)
>  {
> -	for (; nr_pages--; pfn++)
> -		free_frozen_pages(pfn_to_page(pfn), 0);
> +	__free_contig_range_common(pfn, nr_pages, true);

Does this code leak the remaining pages in the contiguous range if a single
page fails preparation when is_frozen is true?

Looking at the implementation of __free_contig_range_common(), the can_free
boolean is initialized to true prior to the loop, but when is_frozen is
true, it is not reset at the beginning of each iteration:

bool can_free = true;
...
for (...) {
    if (!is_frozen)
        can_free = put_page_testzero(page);

    if (can_free && !free_pages_prepare(page, 0))
        can_free = false;
    ...
}

If free_pages_prepare() returns false for a single page (for example, due
to HWPoison), can_free becomes false. Because the reset assignment is
bypassed when is_frozen is true, can_free remains false for all subsequent
loop iterations. Won't this cause every valid page following the failed
page in the contiguous range to be silently skipped and leaked?

Additionally, is it safe to use pointer arithmetic (page++) to iterate over
the contiguous PFN range in __free_contig_range_common()?

In configurations with CONFIG_SPARSEMEM enabled but without
CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP (such as on standard 32-bit systems), struct page
arrays are allocated independently per memory section and are not virtually
contiguous.

When the loop crosses a memory section boundary, page++ increments past the
end of the current section's array into unmapped or unrelated memory. The
code then dereferences this invalid pointer via PageHead(page) and
memdesc_section(page->flags), which could result in an out-of-bounds read.

Furthermore, computing page - start across different memory section
allocations yields undefined behavior and an incorrect batch length for
free_prepared_contig_range().

Should the loop look up each page individually via pfn_to_page(pfn + i)
when memory sections change?

>  }
>  
>  /**

Target Commit:
commit aa8fa415c61507b8e7c4e7a368a371092f7ab661
Author: Muhammad Usama Anjum <usama.anjum@arm.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 27 12:57:15 2026 +0000

    mm/page_alloc: Optimize __free_contig_frozen_range()
    
    Apply the same batch-freeing optimization from free_contig_range() to the
    frozen page path. The previous __free_contig_frozen_range() freed each
    order-0 page individually via free_frozen_pages(), which is slow for the
    same reason the old free_contig_range() was: each page goes to the
    order-0 pcp list rather than being coalesced into higher-order blocks.
    
    Rewrite __free_contig_frozen_range() to call free_pages_prepare() for
    each order-0 page, then batch the prepared pages into the largest
    possible power-of-2 aligned chunks via free_prepared_contig_range().
    If free_pages_prepare() fails (e.g. HWPoison, bad page) the page is
    deliberately not freed; it should not be returned to the allocator.
    
    I've tested CMA through debugfs. The test allocates 16384 pages per
    allocation for several iterations. There is 3.5x improvement.
    
    Before: 1406 usec per iteration
    After:   402 usec per iteration
    
    Before:
    
        70.89%     0.69%  cma              [kernel.kallsyms]      [.] free_contig_frozen_range
                |
                |--70.20%--free_contig_frozen_range
                |          |

                |          |          |

                |          |                     |

                |          |

                |          |
                |          |--7.03%--__preempt_count_dec_and_test
                |          |
                |          |--4.57%--_raw_spin_unlock
                |          |
                |          |--1.96%--__get_pfnblock_flags_mask.isra.0
                |          |
                |           --1.15%--free_frozen_page_commit
                |

    After:
    
        23.57%     0.00%  cma              [kernel.kallsyms]      [.] free_contig_frozen_range
                |
                ---free_contig_frozen_range
                   |
                   |--20.45%--__free_contig_frozen_range
                   |          |
                   |          |--17.77%--free_pages_prepare
                   |          |
                   |           --0.72%--free_prepared_contig_range
                   |                     |
                   |                      --0.55%--__free_frozen_pages
                   |
                    --3.12%--free_pages_prepare
    
    Suggested-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
    Signed-off-by: Muhammad Usama Anjum <usama.anjum@arm.com>

diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
index 9ea2765f5aea..b969192589fb 100644
--- a/mm/page_alloc.c
+++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
@@ -7076,8 +7076,7 @@ static int __alloc_contig_verify_gfp_mask(gfp_t gfp_mask, gfp_t *gfp_cc_mask)
 
 static void __free_contig_frozen_range(unsigned long pfn, unsigned long nr_pages)
 {
-	for (; nr_pages--; pfn++)
-		free_frozen_pages(pfn_to_page(pfn), 0);
+	__free_contig_range_common(pfn, nr_pages, true);
 }

    "thought": "**Critical Bug in Contiguous Page Freeing with Sparse Memory Configurations**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at `__free_contig_range_common` and I see a potential disaster brewing. The core issue revolves around how the code handles `can_free` and how it interacts with the `is_frozen` flag, particularly when dealing with contiguous PFNs in sparse memory configurations (like `CONFIG_SPARSEMEM` without `CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP`).

My initial thought was that `can_free` is initialized to `true`, but that it might not be properly reset within the loop. The code checks `if (!is_frozen)` and only calls `put_page_testzero` and `free_pages_prepare` based on that conditional, but `can_free`'s state is not consistently managed across iterations.

Here's the problem: if `is_frozen` is `true`, `put_page_testzero` is skipped. If `free_pages_prepare` fails in one iteration (setting `can_free` to `false`), the next iteration, with `is_frozen` still `true`, *retains* the `false` value of `can_free`. So, `if (can_free)` is false, and subsequent `free_pages_prepare` calls are skipped. Because `can_free` isn't re-initialized to `true` at the *beginning* of the loop for `is_frozen == true`, a single bad page will cause the *rest* of the range to be silently leaked!  The code never frees them, they're just skipped.

Now, this is a severe bug. A single page that fails to prepare will prevent all subsequent pages in the range from being freed. This is a *huge* memory leak vulnerability!

But then it gets worse. I've realized that the pointer arithmetic `page++` inside the loop, where `page` is a `struct page*`, is a *critical* bug *because* of `CONFIG_SPARSEMEM`. The code assumes contiguous physical frames map to contiguous `struct page` entries. The comment hints at section boundaries, but the code's action isn't safe: `page++` might step outside the valid memory of the section's memmap if the sections are not virtually contiguous, causing a fault or reading garbage. In the absence of `CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP`, we're likely dealing with per-section memmaps where a simple `page++` across a section boundary is undefined behavior. This is because `pfn_to_page` does *not* guarantee contiguity of the returned `struct page` pointers.

The original implementation of related functions (such as `free_contig_range()`) loops with `pfn++` using `pfn_to_page(pfn)` in every iteration, *always* providing a valid address, which fixes this issue.

In this function, though, `__free_contig_range_common` performs `page++` after the initial call to `pfn_to_page`.  Even the comment about `memdesc_section()` is incorrect because the next page pointer isn't valid!

In summary: If you are using sparse memory configuration without virtual memmap (`CONFIG_SPARSEMEM` without `CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP`), this code has:
1.  A severe memory leak, preventing the freeing of subsequent pages in a batch, with `is_frozen`.
2.  A read-out-of-bounds bug: The `page++` might cause a crash or reading garbage if the contiguous physical frames cross section boundaries.
This code needs a serious fix.

476867a6417b095e5ab05c8714d6b2da8cc03436\nAuthor: Ryan Roberts <ryan.roberts@arm.com>\nDate:   Fri Mar 27 12:57:13 2026 +0000\n\n    mm/page_alloc: Optimize free_contig_range()\n    \n    Decompose the range of order-0 pages to be freed into the set of largest\n    possible power-of-2 size and aligned chunks and free them to the pcp or\n    buddy. This improves on the previous approach which freed each order-0\n    page individually in a loop. Testing shows performance to be improved by\n    more than 10x in some cases.\n    \n    Since each page is order-0, we must decrement each page's reference\n    count individually and only consider the page for freeing as part of a\n    high order chunk if the reference count goes to zero. Additionally\n    free_pages_prepare() must be called for each individual order-0 page\n    too, so that the struct page state and global accounting state can be\n    appropriately managed. But once this is done, the resulting high order\n    chunks can be freed as a unit to the pcp or buddy.\n    \n    This significantly speeds up the free operation but also has the side\n    benefit that high order blocks are added to the pcp instead of each page\n    ending up on the pcp order-0 list; memory remains more readily available\n    in high orders.\n    \n    vmalloc will shortly become a user of this new optimized\n    free_contig_range() since it aggressively allocates high order\n    non-compound pages, but then calls split_page() to end up with\n    contiguous order-0 pages. These can now be freed much more efficiently.\n    \n    The execution time of the following function was measured in a server\n    class arm64 machine:\n    \n    static int page_alloc_high_order_test(void)\n    {\n            unsigned int order = HPAGE_PMD_ORDER;\n            struct page *page;\n            int i;\n    \n            for (i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {\n Okay, so I've been digging into this patch, and I've found a pretty nasty bug. It seems that this patch, which refactors `__free_contig_frozen_range` to use `__free_contig_range_common`, introduces a critical memory leak. The problem stems from the interaction between `free_pages_prepare()` and how `__free_contig_range_common` handles frozen pages.

The original code, and even the new function `__free_contig_frozen_range` calls, relies on the behavior of `free_pages_prepare()`, which can *intentionally* return `false` in certain scenarios. The intent is that a failed preparation shouldn't result in that specific page being returned to the allocator. This makes sense. The author clearly points this out in the commit message. The issue, however, is that when dealing with *frozen* pages (`is_frozen == true`), the code's logic goes sideways.

Here's the breakdown. `__free_contig_range_common` uses a `can_free` boolean to track whether a page can be freed. The loop logic works like this:

1.  `can_free` is initialized to `true` at the beginning of the loop.
2.  If it's *not* a frozen page, `put_page_testzero()` is called, and the result updates `can_free`.
3.  `free_pages_prepare()` is called. If it returns `false` (e.g., HWPoison, bad page), `can_free` is set to `false`.
4.  If `can_free` is false, it means `free_pages_prepare` failed, so it skips the current page and continues to the next one.

The flaw is in the *next* iteration when `is_frozen` is `true`. `put_page_testzero()` is *skipped* and `can_free` is *not* reset. Therefore, if `free_pages_prepare()` fails on one page, `can_free` becomes `false`, and every *subsequent* page in the contiguous range is also skipped, because `can_free` remains `false` and `free_pages_prepare` is *never* called again. This means those pages are never prepared and therefore *never freed*. They're leaked. This totally breaks the expected behavior.

So, the issue is that once `free_pages_prepare` fails on a frozen page, the `can_free` variable is *stateful* across iterations of the loop, resulting in a leak. This perfectly fits the description of an "unhandled corner case related to the feature's logic." The author understands `free_pages_prepare` can fail, but missed this subtle logic error.

I've checked the safety of `free_pages_prepare` with frozen pages – it’s fine, because a frozen page has a refcount of 0, which is what `free_pages_prepare` expects. The iteration using `page++` is also safe. `free_pages_prepare` can also modify page flags, but that's not the problem here. There's no other assignment to `can_free`, so if it becomes `false`, it stays `false`. The patch looks to have refactored existing logic to reuse code. The problem is definitely related to this patch because it triggers this particular execution path.

The crucial point is that this patch introduces the use of this flawed path when `is_frozen` is `true` through the use of `__free_contig_range_common`. The commit message also clearly spells out that `free_pages_prepare` is intended for each order-0 page. That's the part that is broken.

With `SPARSEMEM` and *no* `VMEMMAP`, the `struct page` arrays are allocated per section. The section size is `PAGES_PER_SECTION`. Accessing `page->flags` would then be a classic out-of-bounds array access, a potential memory corruption issue!

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `__free_contig_range_common` Function with `is_frozen = true`**

Okay, so I'm looking at this `__free_contig_range_common` function, specifically when `is_frozen` is `true`.  The goal here is to free a contiguous range of pages. Initially, `can_free` is set to `true`.  The function iterates through each page in the range.  If `is_frozen` is `false`, it calls `put_page_testzero` on the page and then `free_pages_prepare`. If `is_frozen` is `true`, `put_page_testzero` is skipped.

My immediate thought is, if `free_pages_prepare` returns `false` (indicating a problem with the page, like HWPoison), `can_free` becomes `false`. The key realization is that since `is_frozen` is true, the `can_free` variable is *not* reset to `true` at the beginning of each loop iteration. This is the crucial point!

In the subsequent iterations, because `is_frozen` is `true`, `can_free = put_page_testzero(page);` is *completely* skipped.  Therefore, if `free_pages_prepare` returns `false` in any iteration, `can_free` gets stuck as `false` and *never* becomes true again.

Consequently, `free_pages_prepare` will never be called on any of the *subsequent* pages in the contiguous range because the `if (can_free)` block is skipped.  This means those pages will be leaked – never freed or prepared!  The loop will just continue to the next page, but `can_free` will always remain `false`.

So, the issue arises due to the conditional skipping of `put_page_testzero` when `is_frozen` is true, causing `can_free` to retain its `false` state after the first error, and preventing subsequent page freeing.  The logic relies on `can_free` being updated in *every* iteration, which is not what happens when the function is frozen. The state of `can_free` is not properly handled in this frozen state.

The callers of `__free_contig_frozen_range` are within the error paths of functions like `alloc_contig_frozen_range`, or as part of tail-freeing logic, and also by `free_contig_frozen_range` directly.

The core of the problem lies in the `__free_contig_range_common` function. Specifically, inside the loop that iterates through the pages, there's a critical flaw in how the `can_free` boolean is handled when `is_frozen` is true.

Here's the breakdown: When `is_frozen` is true, `can_free` is initialized outside the loop, but it's *never* reset to `true` inside the loop.  If `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)` returns false for any reason (say, the page is poisoned or otherwise bad), `can_free` becomes `false`. Because `can_free` is not reset inside the loop and since the code skips `put_page_testzero` when `is_frozen` is true, *all* subsequent pages in the contiguous range are then skipped because `can_free` remains `false`. The `continue` statement prevents any further processing for those pages.

This means that a single bad page can cause an entire contiguous range to leak memory if it's frozen. That's a major bug. In the `is_frozen == true` case, each page *should* be assumed free, but this code is not behaving that way.

Interestingly, this logic was introduced in a patch that, if I read correctly, either introduced `__free_contig_range_common` or significantly altered its behavior.  The commit message claims to be applying a batch-freeing optimization. The commit message says "Apply the same batch-freeing optimization from free_contig_range() to the frozen page path. The previous __free_contig_frozen_range() freed each order-0 page individually via free_frozen_pages()... Rewrite __free_contig_frozen_range() to call free_pages_prepare() for each order-0 page..."

It seems like this bug stems from the fact that `__free_contig_range_common` was refactored in this same patch (or earlier) to take an `is_frozen` parameter, but the implications for the `can_free` variable were not fully considered. The patch, intended to optimize the free path, unfortunately introduced this critical leak. The intent seems to be to batch free pages, but this implementation does not do that correctly in the case that `is_frozen` is set to true.

We're looking at a memory leak here. I need to get this reported.

This means `can_free` stays `false`.

Then the `if (can_free)` block is skipped and `if (!can_free)` gets triggered. And, yes, it looks like this will leak *all* subsequent pages.

```c
		if (can_free)

```

    "thought": "**Analyzing the `__free_pages_prepare` Function and its Implications**

Okay, so I'm looking at `__free_pages_prepare` and it's clear: if the `FPI_PREPARED` flag is set, it just bails out immediately, returning `true`. That makes total sense, because if you're calling `free_prepared_contig_range`, it means the pages have already been "prepared." `__free_contig_range_common` already calls `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)` for individual order-0 pages, and then bundles them up to pass to the function that uses the `FPI_PREPARED` flag. It then calls `__free_frozen_pages` which is also meant to skip further prep on the page, as it's been prepared via `FPI_PREPARED`.

So, that confirms it: the issue with `can_free` when `is_frozen` is `true` is definitely a real problem! It's a significant bug. I need to make a note of this.

But, hold on... are there *other* problems here? Let's dig deeper. What exactly does `is_frozen == true` *mean* in the context of `__free_contig_range_common`? If `is_frozen` is `true`, it means the pages are ALREADY frozen, and therefore their refcount is already zero. Which means `put_page_testzero` – which checks if the refcount drops to zero – would almost certainly cause an underflow, or trigger a BUG, because it's *already* at zero. We have to skip that call. But... we still need to make sure to initialize `can_free = true` on each iteration of the loop.

Wait, the next question is: was `is_frozen == true` ever even used before this patch? I should go back and review the callers of `__free_contig_range_common` before this patch was introduced to fully understand the landscape.

This improves on the previous approach which freed each order-0\n    page individually in a loop. Testing shows performance to be improved by\n    more than 10x in some cases.\n    \n    Since each page is order-0, we must decrement each page's reference\n    count individually and only consider the page for freeing as part of a\n    high order chunk if the reference count goes to zero. Additionally\n    free_pages_prepare() must be called for each individual order-0 page\n    too, so that the struct page state and global accounting state can be\n    appropriately managed. But once this is done, the resulting high order\n    chunks can be freed as a unit to the pcp or buddy.\n    \n    This significantly speeds up the free operation but also has the side\n    benefit that high order blocks are added to the pcp instead of each page\n    ending up on the pcp order-0 list; memory remains more readily available\n    in high orders.\n    \n    vmalloc will shortly become a user of this new optimized\n    free_contig_range() since it aggressively allocates high order\n    non-compound pages, but then calls split_page() to end up with\n    contiguous order-0 pages. Okay, so I'm diving into this contiguous page freeing logic, and the core of my thought process is: what happens if a page free operation fails, and how does that affect subsequent pages in a range? I see that `__free_pages_prepare`, `trace_mm_page_free`, and `kmsan_free_page` are involved. The initial scenario is about freeing an order-3 chunk. If `free_prepared_contig_range` creates an order-3 chunk, and frees it via `__free_frozen_pages(page, 3, FPI_PREPARED)`, it sends order 3 to the buddy. This seems fine, since frozen pages are aligned and contiguous, it's just an optimization.

I start scrutinizing `free_one_page`, making sure it's handling this chunking. It's handling order > 0 just fine. The buddy allocator's handling of order-3 pages, and the optimization Ryan Roberts introduced, seem safe, given the memory alignment. I'm satisfied with that part.

Now I'm focusing on `__free_contig_range_common`. I’m looking at how it handles the loop and `is_frozen`. Is `put_page_testzero` going to do something important? No, frozen pages have refcount 0. I focus in on `can_free`, and I see a potential problem.

```c
	bool can_free = true;
	unsigned long i;

	for (i = 0; i < nr_pages; i++, page++) {
		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(PageHead(page));
		VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(PageTail(page));

		if (!is_frozen)
			can_free = put_page_testzero(page);

		if (can_free)
			can_free = free_pages_prepare(page, 0);

		if (!can_free) {
			if (start) {
				free_prepared_contig_range(start, page - start);
				start = NULL;
			}
			continue;
		}
```

If `is_frozen` is true, the `can_free` flag isn't reset. If a single page fails `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)`, the flag becomes false, and every subsequent page skips `free_pages_prepare()` and just `continue`. They will be leaked. No other case exists where `can_free` could be false on entry to the loop body. The bug is that `can_free` must be reset to `true` for `is_frozen == true` pages.

The code seems to correctly chunk pages via `free_prepared_contig_range`, especially handling the case where `nr_pages` is large and chunking via `order`. The potential `pfn` = 0 case, and it's use of `__ffs` is handled. The case of a non-power-of-two page is also safe.

Then, I double-check `memdesc_section` and its use in `__free_contig_range_common`. I'm verifying the section of code with the start_sec handling. The `start_sec` comparison using `memdesc_section(page->flags)` looks correct, since `start` is set to null, and will be reinitialized, given the bug in `can_free` is fixed.

Now I'm confident about the `can_free` bug. I walk through the logic step-by-step to be sure. I've broken the logic down, and can see the bug clearly: a persistent `can_free = false` state causes all subsequent pages to be skipped, leading to a silent memory leak. This satisfies the prompt.

Are there any other bugs? `memdesc_section` seems safe to call. I'm satisfied with the assessment.

    "thought": "**Concurrency and Memory Leak Analysis of Contiguous Page Freeing**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about the interaction between `__free_pages_prepare` and `__free_frozen_pages`. I see that the `FPI_PREPARED` flag is handled correctly, ensuring that prepared pages are bypassed, which is good. The key issue appears to be a potential memory leak.

Initially, I went through the old code that iterated through pages and called `free_frozen_pages`. I noted that `free_frozen_pages` calls `__free_pages_prepare`, which can cause a page to be deliberately *not* freed (e.g., HWPoison scenarios). The new code uses a common function `__free_contig_range_common`. I found a bug: In `__free_contig_frozen_range` (when `is_frozen` is `true`), `can_free` becomes `false` for a page, it *remains* false for all subsequent pages in the contiguous range. This is a critical memory leak! It's a state-persistence issue.

I then shifted my focus to concurrency. I need to make sure I am not missing any locking issues. The prompt emphasizes this, and it *is* my expertise, after all. The potential for a concurrency issue with `put_page_testzero` crossed my mind. The function *does* involve refcounts. It drops a reference to a page and tests if the refcount is zero. In the non-frozen case (`__free_contig_range` where `is_frozen` is `false`), it *does* make sense, because the page might still have references. The assumption is that the caller *should* own all the pages. If `put_page_testzero` returns false, it means another reference exists, and the page is skipped. But now I'm concerned about the reference that was just dropped, but the caller isn't informed it wasn't freed. But `put_page_testzero` does an atomic dec-and-test, so it is handled correctly.

The critical thing, I realized, is that this entire patch *only* modifies `__free_contig_frozen_range` to use the common function. It doesn't modify the common function itself.

Now I am absolutely sure: The bug stems from the fact that `can_free` persists in the frozen context, and that the author only changed the `__free_contig_frozen_range`! The common function itself is not modified. I need to emphasize this memory leak, this state-persistence bug, to the fullest extent. This is the critical finding.

    "content": "{"content":"commit 476867a6417b095e5ab05c8714d6b2da8cc03436\nAuthor: Ryan Roberts <ryan.roberts@arm.com>\nDate:   Fri Mar 27 12:57:13 2026 +0000\n\n    mm/page_alloc: Optimize free_contig_range()\n    \n    Decompose the range of order-0 pages to be freed into the set of largest\n    possible power-of-2 size and aligned chunks and free them to the pcp or\n    buddy. This improves on the previous approach which freed each order-0\n    page individually in a loop. Testing shows performance to be improved by\n    more than 10x in some cases.\n    \n    Since each page is order-0, we must decrement each page's reference\n    count individually and only consider the page for freeing as part of a\n    high order chunk if the reference count goes to zero. Additionally\n    free_pages_prepare() must be called for each individual order-0 page\n    too, so that the struct page state and global accounting state can be\n    appropriately managed. But once this is done, the resulting high order\n    chunks can be freed as a unit to the pcp or buddy.\n    \n    This significantly speeds up the free operation but also has the side\n    benefit that high order blocks are added to the pcp instead of each page\n    ending up on the pcp order-0 list; memory remains more readily available\n    in high orders.\n    \n    vmalloc will shortly become a user of this new optimized\n    free_contig_range() since it aggressively allocates high order\n    non-compound pages, but then calls split_page() to end up with\n    contiguous order-0 pages. These can now be freed much more efficiently.\n    \n    The execution time of the following function was measured in a server\n    class arm64 machine:\n    \n    static int     "thought": "**Bug Found! A Significant Memory Leak in Contiguous Frozen Page Freeing**

Okay, this is concerning. I was reviewing this patch by Muhammad Usama Anjum and Ryan Roberts, specifically the optimization to `__free_contig_frozen_range()` (patch `aa8fa415c615 mm/page_alloc: Optimize __free_contig_frozen_range()`), and I've stumbled upon a serious bug. It seems that by changing `__free_contig_frozen_range` to use `__free_contig_range_common(..., true)`, this optimization introduces a memory leak in the frozen page freeing path, a leak that is especially problematic in CMA.

The problem lies within `__free_contig_range_common()`, where the `is_frozen=true` logic is used. In a previous patch, with `is_frozen=false`, `put_page_testzero` reset the `can_free` flag within the loop, ensuring that the function could correctly handle errors during page freeing. However, when `is_frozen=true`, this `put_page_testzero` check is skipped. If `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)` fails on a particular page (due to HWPoison or a bad page, for example), `can_free` is set to false, but the subsequent iterations *do not* re-evaluate `can_free` via `put_page_testzero(page)`. So all subsequent pages in that contiguous range are *silently* leaked. They're skipped. This breaks memory management for frozen pages!

Here's how it breaks down:
1.  The loop in `__free_contig_range_common()` iterates through pages. The variable `can_free` determines if a page should be freed.
2.  When `is_frozen` is true, the `can_free = put_page_testzero(page)` check is skipped.
3.  If `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)` fails, `can_free` is set to `false`.
4.  In the next iteration (and all subsequent iterations), the skipped `put_page_testzero` is not run. `can_free` remains false.
5.  Thus, `free_pages_prepare` will never be called again, so the pages are silently leaked.

Now, because `free_pages_prepare` *can* return false (this is intentionally designed behavior), this leak can easily occur. In this case, this is exactly what the commit message itself alludes to. By not resetting `can_free` in the `is_frozen=true` mode, we've broken the loop.

Okay, let's look at the locking considerations. It looks like the program returns to the main loop if `free_pages_prepare` fails, and continues.
The code does:
```c
			if (start) {
				free_prepared_contig_range(start, page - start);
				start = NULL;
			}
			continue;
```
If `start` is set, the function outputs a contiguous range of successfully freed pages, and then returns.

This means that a failure *anywhere* in the contiguous range causes a leak of *all* remaining pages, until the next successful range.

The more I think about it, the worse it gets. Since `free_contig_frozen_range` is used heavily in CMA, we are looking at potential leaks of up to 16384 pages (as per the commit message testing), if a single page fails preparation!

I need to report this immediately. This is a serious problem.

I did briefly consider some potential locking issues, specifically with `free_pages_prepare` failing and the resulting `continue;`, but I don't see any immediate concerns there.

However, I've just had a moment. I was looking at `__free_frozen_pages` which is called in `free_prepared_contig_range()`. There's a check for `is_migrate_isolate(migratetype)` within it. This calls `free_one_page()` with an underlying lock.

I just went through and traced it.
Specifically, `__free_frozen_pages` now *does not* hold a lock when calling `free_one_page`!
So, there are no locking concerns in this path.

Alright, I'm confident. This optimization has introduced a significant memory leak and needs immediate attention. I'm going to report this.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Contiguous Frozen Page Freeing Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. My primary concern, as identified initially, remains the memory leak within `__free_contig_range_common` when `is_frozen` is true, and I'm confident that the lack of `can_free` reset is the core of the problem. This fully aligns with the directive.

I've traced the code path, and the change from the original loop with `free_frozen_pages` to the current structure, calling `__free_contig_range_common`, is where the issue lies. The key point is that `__free_contig_range_common` now calls `free_pages_prepare()` or `__free_pages_prepare` (which effectively are the same). However, with the `is_frozen = true` condition, once `free_pages_prepare()` returns false for a page, the subsequent pages in the contiguous range are never re-evaluated for their `can_free` status. This leads to a scenario where all subsequent pages are silently skipped, meaning that once a single page fails preparation, the entire contiguous block is leaked.

To be precise, I can summarize this as:

*   **Type:** Memory Leak / Logic Bug
*   **Description:** The loop in `__free_contig_range_common()` silently leaks all remaining pages in the batch if `free_pages_prepare()` fails while `is_frozen` is true.
*   **Reasoning:** When `__free_contig_frozen_range()` calls `__free_contig_range_common(..., true)`, the `is_frozen` parameter is true. In the processing loop, `put_page_testzero()` is skipped, meaning `can_free` is not re-evaluated per page. If `free_pages_prepare()` returns false for any page (e.g., HWPoison), `can_free` becomes false. For all subsequent pages in the loop, `can_free` remains false because `put_page_testzero()` is bypassed, preventing `free_pages_prepare()` from running. Consequently, the loop skips freeing all subsequent pages in the contiguous block, resulting in a severe memory leak (up to thousands of pages).

Now, let's explore other potential issues. I've considered potential concurrency or locking problems. Specifically, I looked at the use of `pcp_spin_trylock` and `pcp_spin_lock` within `__free_frozen_pages`, and that is safe. No apparent RCU violations or memory barrier issues here. The code also does not sleep in an atomic context, and it uses existing locking mechanisms correctly within the `free_prepared_contig_range` function.

Next, I examined the logic within `free_prepared_contig_range` related to buddy allocator compatibility. The code calculates the maximum buddy order based on the page's PFN and the total number of pages to free. The code determines `order = pfn ? __ffs(pfn) : MAX_PAGE_ORDER;`, which ensures alignment. The logic to handle potential fragmentation due to `free_pages_prepare()` failing in the middle of a block is crucial. If the preparation fails, the code splits the contiguous range and continues, and the `start` is correctly updated. So, while my initial memory leak concern exists and is critical, the code attempts to manage these splits correctly.

Finally, the use of `start_sec` to handle memory section changes is correct. It correctly breaks batches when `memdesc_section` changes. Also, I evaluated the interaction between `order > 0` and `FPI_PREPARED` when calling `__free_frozen_pages`. I traced that `__free_pages_ok` calls `free_one_page(zone, page, pfn, order, fpi_flags);` and determined this part of the code is safe. Specifically, the prior loop calls `free_pages_prepare(page, 0)` on each page.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Memory Corruption and Leaks in Contiguous Memory Management**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial assessment centers on two primary concerns. First, in `__free_contig_range_common()`, a crucial logical flaw appears when `is_frozen` is true. Looking at the code, it's pretty clear: if a single `free_pages_prepare()` call fails within the loop, the `can_free` flag effectively latches to `false`. Subsequent pages within the contiguous range are then silently skipped, leading to a memory leak. The `put_page_testzero()` call is bypassed, and crucially, `can_free` *isn't* reset to `true` at the start of each iteration when `is_frozen` is active. This makes `can_free` act as a latch, which is definitely the root cause. This is a high-priority issue as it directly results in memory leakage. A pretty serious bug, in fact.

Second, the use of pointer arithmetic (`page++`) to iterate over a contiguous range of PFNs raises concerns about potential out-of-bounds memory access and undefined behavior. I need to clarify that the comment does make mention of this concern. Specifically, the comment highlights that "contiguous PFNs might not have a contiguous `struct pages`". This means that using `page++` in a naive fashion across memory section boundaries is a potential problem. If the underlying `struct page` representations aren't physically contiguous, incrementing the pointer could lead to accessing memory outside the intended allocation or, worse, pointing to an unrelated PFN. Need to look into what other kernel functions might be handling this situation.

mm/page_alloc: Optimize __free_contig_frozen_range()

This commit optimizes the frozen page freeing path by replacing the
individual page freeing loop with a call to __free_contig_range_common().
This aims to improve performance by batching and coalescing prepared
pages into higher-order blocks.

> diff --git a/mm/page_alloc.c b/mm/page_alloc.c
> index 9ea2765f5aea..b969192589fb 100644
> --- a/mm/page_alloc.c
> +++ b/mm/page_alloc.c
> @@ -7076,8 +7076,7 @@ static int __alloc_contig_verify_gfp_mask(gfp_t gfp_mask, gfp_t *gfp_cc_mask)
>  
>  static void __free_contig_frozen_range(unsigned long pfn, unsigned long nr_pages)
>  {
> -	for (; nr_pages--; pfn++)
> -		free_frozen_pages(pfn_to_page(pfn), 0);
> +	__free_contig_range_common(pfn, nr_pages, true);

Does this code leak the remaining pages in the contiguous range if a single
page fails preparation when is_frozen is true?

Looking at the implementation of __free_contig_range_common(), the can_free
boolean is initialized to true prior to the loop, but when is_frozen is
true, it is not reset at the beginning of each iteration:

bool can_free = true;
...
for (...) {
    if (!is_frozen)
        can_free = put_page_testzero(page);

    if (can_free && !free_pages_prepare(page, 0))
        can_free = false;
    ...
}

If free_pages_prepare() returns false for a single page (for example, due
to HWPoison), can_free becomes false. Because the reset assignment is
bypassed when is_frozen is true, can_free remains false for all subsequent
loop iterations. Won't this cause every valid page following the failed
page in the contiguous range to be silently skipped and leaked?

Additionally, is it safe to use pointer arithmetic (page++) to iterate over
the contiguous PFN range in __free_contig_range_common()?

In configurations with CONFIG_SPARSEMEM enabled but without
CONFIG_SPARSEMEM_VMEMMAP (such as on standard 32-bit systems), struct page
arrays are allocated independently per memory section and are not virtually
contiguous.

When the loop crosses a memory section boundary, page++ increments past the
end of the current section's array into unmapped or unrelated memory. The
code then dereferences this invalid pointer via PageHead(page) and
memdesc_section(page->flags), which could result in an out-of-bounds read.

Furthermore, computing page - start across different memory section
allocations yields undefined behavior and an incorrect batch length for
free_prepared_contig_range().

Should the loop look up each page individually via pfn_to_page(pfn + i)
when memory sections change?
